notes: Single-pass blind run of the haberlah/wardley-mapping skill on the energy-disruption scenario (July 2022). Executed SKILL.md Steps 1-7 verbatim from /workspaces/wardleymap_math_model/skills/wardley-map-workspace/competitor-compare/skills/wardley-map-workspace/competitor-compare/haberlah/skill/ (note: the task stated path haberlah/skill/ was a nested location; the actual skill tree was one level deeper — used it as-is). Answered Step 1 strategic-context questions from the scenario itself (no user clarification available); built a 30-component value chain (3 anchors: Consumer Today, Consumer Tomorrow, Society & Government; 27 non-anchor covering the generation mix, P/T/D chain, storage, market layer, consumer apparatus, and control/regulation/CO2 stack); used haberlah's own evolution ranges (Genesis 0.00-0.17, Custom-Built 0.18-0.39, Product 0.40-0.69, Commodity 0.70-1.00) per references/evolution-assessment.md; did Step 4 manual validation (positions in [0,1], no cycles, no close pairs <0.06 on both axes, all evolve targets rightward, every referenced component exists); additionally enforced Wardley-math ν(a) ≥ ν(b) on every dependency (stricter than haberlah's own validator). Produced all three deliverables per skill contract: React/SVG artifact embedded in output.md, raw OWM block in draft.owm + duplicated in output.md, strategic commentary with per-component rationale table (30 rows), 6 key strategic observations, doctrine check against 6 principles, 5 recommended actions, build-vs-buy table (9 rows), 12-24 month evolution predictions (7 evolutions), and 3 open questions. Divergences from skill workflow: (1) Step 2 recommends 8-15 components; carried 30 because the brief explicitly names 5 generation types + 4 storage types + 3 T/D stages + 5 market pieces + 4 consumer-facing pieces + 3 control/regulation pieces + capital intensity + supply-chain awareness + 3 anchors. Splitting into submaps would fragment the central narrative (the gap between the mature commoditised supply side and the immature externality/tomorrow side), which is the map's strategic point. Noted and accepted the readability cost. (2) Used 3 anchors rather than 1 because the scenario's own today-vs-tomorrow framing requires it to show evolution tension; OWM grammar supports multi-anchor. (3) No existing OWM input, so skipped parse_owm.py branch. (4) Did not run validate_map.py (blind first-pass); did the manual checks it specifies. No access to precise token/time telemetry in this harness.
